title: Web Search Engine — System Design
description: Design a web search engine: crawl the web, build an inverted index over billions of documents, and answer keyword queries with ranked results in well under a second. The themes are the crawl pipeline, inverted-index construction, document-sharded scatter/gather serving, and ranking.

nodes.label: Crawler Fleet
nodes.description: Fetches web pages, respecting politeness and robots rules.

nodes.label: URL Frontier
nodes.description: Prioritized, politeness-aware queue of URLs to crawl.

nodes.label: Seen-URL / Dedup Store
nodes.description: Tracks crawled/known URLs and content hashes to avoid re-crawl and dupes.

nodes.label: Indexing Pipeline
nodes.description: Parses docs, extracts terms/links, builds inverted-index segments.

nodes.label: Inverted Index Shards
nodes.description: Document-partitioned posting lists; the searchable index.

nodes.label: Link Graph / Page Scores
nodes.description: Web link graph and precomputed query-independent scores (e.g. PageRank).

nodes.label: Query Service
nodes.description: Parses the query and scatters it to index shards (aggregator).

nodes.label: Ranking Service
nodes.description: Scores candidate docs by relevance + page score.

nodes.label: Doc / Snippet Cache
nodes.description: Hot document metadata and snippets for result hydration.

nodes.label: Searcher
nodes.description: Issues queries and reads ranked results.

functional: Crawl the web continuously and keep the index fresh.
Answer keyword queries with a ranked list of relevant documents.
Return titles, URLs, and snippets for results.
Support multi-term queries and basic operators.
nonFunctional: Query latency p99 well under a second across billions of documents.
Crawl politely (robots.txt, rate limits per host).
Index freshness: new/changed content appears within a bounded time.
Scale storage and serving horizontally over the whole corpus.

capacity.label: Documents
capacity.note: Drives index size and shard count.

capacity.label: Index size
capacity.note: 100B docs x ~5KB parsed content -> ~500TB raw text; postings + positions + per-doc metadata roughly equal the text, so the searchable index is ~PB-class and the doc store plus raw HTML pushes the total into tens of PB.

capacity.label: Shard count
capacity.note: Target ~100GB-200GB of postings per shard so each shard's working set stays in RAM/SSD; 100B docs / 10M per shard ~= 10K primary shards.

capacity.label: Replicas
capacity.note: Replicas serve QPS and survive shard loss; total serving fleet ~= 10K shards x 3 = ~30K index servers (plus more replicas for hot shards).

capacity.label: Queries per second
capacity.note: Assume a ~60-70% result-cache hit rate on skewed query traffic, so only ~30-40K QPS actually scatter to shards.

capacity.label: Scatter fan-out
capacity.note: Cache-miss QPS x shard count — absorbed by a broker fan-out tree, not one box. Each replica set sees ~30K req/s; with 3 replicas that is ~10K req/s per index server, which sets the per-server CPU/IO budget (see step 4b).

capacity.label: Per-query work
capacity.note: Each shard returns a small top-k; the broker merges 10K x top-k into a global candidate set, then phase-2 re-ranks only the top ~1000. Network: ~10K shards x ~1KB top-k ~= ~10MB gathered per cache-miss query.

capacity.label: Deadline budget
capacity.note: ~Few ms broker, ~100-200ms shard fan-out (bounded by slowest replica + hedging), ~50-100ms merge/re-rank, ~tens of ms hydration. Shards that miss the deadline are dropped -> partial results rather than a timeout.

capacity.label: Crawl rate
capacity.note: ~1B fetches/day ~= ~12K fetches/s sustained; at ~2MB avg page that is ~25GB/s ~= 200Gbps of ingress, before per-host politeness caps cut effective throughput.

api.description: Run a query and return ranked results. q supports multi-term, quoted phrases ("exact match"), and basic boolean operators (+required, -excluded, OR). hl/gl set language and country for ranking/locale; num+cursor page results — cursors are opaque, short-lived (minutes), and deep pagination is capped because the index changes under you. freshness is a ranking bias/filter toward recent content, not a guarantee that all new pages are visible. approxTotal is an estimate (capped at web scale, not an exact count). Under deadline pressure the response can be partial: partialResults flags it, timedOutShards counts dropped shards, servedFromCache tells whether the result cache answered.

dataModel.name: documents
dataModel.note: Per-doc metadata and content for snippets/ranking.

dataModel.name: doc_versions
dataModel.note: Content lifecycle per doc: each crawl that materially changes content is a new version.

fields.name: segment_id
fields.type: string (which index segment carries this version)

dataModel.name: index_segments
dataModel.note: Immutable index units; serving reads the published set.

fields.name: generation
fields.type: bigint (monotonic; readers refresh to latest published)

dataModel.name: url_frontier
dataModel.note: Crawl queue state, per canonical URL.

fields.name: host
fields.type: string FK -> host_crawl_state (politeness + robots scheduled per host)

dataModel.name: host_crawl_state
dataModel.note: Per-host politeness and robots state — the frontier schedules eligible hosts, then URLs within them.

fields.name: crawl_delay
fields.type: float (per-host rate limit / min gap between fetches)

fields.name: next_host_fetch_at
fields.type: timestamp (when this host may be fetched again)

fields.name: backoff_state
fields.type: enum(ok, slowed, blocked) + recent 429/5xx error budget

dataModel.name: serving_metrics (control plane)
dataModel.note: Time-series the system is operated by — emitted across crawl, index, serving, and ranking; alerts fire on these.

fields.name: crawl
fields.type: frontier backlog by host/priority, fetch success rate, robots/blocked rate, 429/5xx rate, duplicate rate, crawl lag

fields.name: indexing
fields.type: segment build lag, validation failures, publish generation, merge backlog, tombstone ratio, rollback count

fields.name: serving
fields.type: result-cache hit rate, doc-cache hit rate, cache-miss QPS, shard p95/p99, hedge rate, partial-result rate, timeout rate, candidate count, merge/rank latency

fields.name: quality
fields.type: spam demotion rate, suppression count, ranker model version

patterns.name: Politeness-aware crawl frontier
patterns.what: A prioritized URL queue that respects robots.txt and per-host rate limits.
patterns.whenToUse: Crawling at scale without hammering or getting blocked by sites.
patterns.group: Crawl the Web with a URL Frontier

patterns.name: Inverted index
patterns.what: Map each term to the list of documents (and positions) containing it.
patterns.whenToUse: Fast keyword lookup over a large corpus.

patterns.name: Document partitioning (sharding)
patterns.what: Split the index by document so each shard holds full postings for its docs.
patterns.whenToUse: Corpora too large for one machine; enables parallel scatter/gather.
patterns.group: Shard the Index and Scatter-Gather Queries

patterns.name: Scatter-gather query
patterns.what: Send the query to all shards, collect top-k from each, merge and re-rank.
patterns.whenToUse: Document-partitioned indexes where any doc can match.
patterns.group: Shard the Index and Scatter-Gather Queries

patterns.name: Two-phase ranking
patterns.what: Cheap candidate retrieval per shard, then an expensive re-rank of the merged candidates.
patterns.whenToUse: When full ranking over all matches is too costly.

patterns.name: Result + doc caching
patterns.what: Cache popular query results and hot document snippets.
patterns.whenToUse: Skewed query/document popularity (a few queries dominate).

steps.title: 1. Naive Baseline: Scan Every Document for the Query Terms
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. Assume pages have already been fetched somehow (the next step builds the crawler) and stored in a document store; on each search read through every document checking whether it contains the query terms, collecting the matches.

This is correct and trivial — and it is hopeless at web scale. A single query would have to scan 100B+ documents (petabytes), so even one search takes far longer than the sub-second budget, and serving ~100K queries/sec this way is physically impossible. The cost is linear in the corpus size for every single query.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: instead of scanning documents per query, build an inverted index once (term -> the documents containing it) so a query becomes a posting-list lookup, then shard that index across machines and scatter-gather queries to stay sub-second at scale.

view.caption: The searcher's query hits the query service, which scans every document in the document store looking for the terms.

steps.decisionPrompt: If each search scans every stored document for the query terms, how long does one query take over 100B docs — and what about 100K of them per second?
whyNow: Establishing the scan-every-document baseline first makes it obvious why an inverted index exists and why it must be sharded and served via scatter-gather — every later step removes a specific pain from here.

recap.after: Pages are stored; each search linearly scans every document for the query terms.
recap.newRisk: Per-query cost is linear in the whole corpus — infeasible at billions of docs and ~100K QPS.

traps.trap: Answering queries by scanning the document corpus per request.
traps.why: Per-query work scales with corpus size, so latency and cost are impossible at web scale.
traps.instead: Build an inverted index once so a query is a posting-list lookup; shard it and scatter-gather for scale.

steps.title: 2. Crawl the Web with a URL Frontier
description: A crawler fleet fetches pages, but a naive breadth-first crawl won't do: you must be polite (obey robots.txt, rate-limit per host), avoid re-fetching unchanged pages, and prioritize important/fresh URLs.

A URL frontier is a prioritized, politeness-aware queue: it hands out URLs respecting per-host delays and priority. Crawlers fetch, store the raw page, dedup by URL and content hash, and enqueue newly discovered links.

Production crawling needs more than fetch-and-store. Canonicalize URLs (strip tracking params, resolve redirects) so the same page isn't crawled under many URLs; cache robots.txt per host so you don't refetch it every request; isolate DNS/fetch with timeouts; retry transient failures with exponential backoff and host-level backoff on 429/5xx; cluster near-duplicate content by hash; detect crawler traps (infinite calendars, faceted-URL explosions) and cap depth/URLs per host; and seed from sitemaps. A recrawl policy driven by each page's observed change frequency decides who gets re-fetched soon vs. rarely.

Underpinning all of this is a seen-URL/content store: it keys on both the canonical URL and a content hash, so it prevents re-crawling the same page and catches the same content served under different URLs.

view.caption: The URL frontier feeds URLs to the crawler fleet, which dedups against the seen-URL store, stores raw pages in the document store, and enqueues newly discovered links back to the frontier.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you crawl billions of pages fast without overwhelming any single website?

concepts.definition: A prioritized, politeness-bounded queue of URLs to crawl.
concepts.whyItMatters: Balances coverage, freshness, and politeness; the heart of a scalable crawler.
concepts.example: Per-host buckets with next_fetch_at delays + priority by importance/freshness.
concepts.group: Crawl the Web with a URL Frontier

whyNow: Crawling is the data source; a politeness-aware frontier is what makes large-scale crawling both effective and acceptable to the web.

recap.after: A growing, deduped corpus of fetched pages.
recap.newRisk: Raw pages aren't searchable — they must be indexed.

traps.trap: Naive BFS crawling without per-host limits.
traps.why: You DDoS sites, get blocked, and waste budget re-fetching unchanged pages.
traps.instead: A prioritized frontier with politeness and content-hash dedup.

traps.trap: Treating every distinct URL as a distinct page.
traps.why: Tracking params, session IDs, and infinite faceted/calendar URLs explode the frontier and bloat the index with duplicates (crawler traps).
traps.instead: Canonicalize URLs, cluster by content hash, and cap depth/URLs per host.

failureDrills.scenario: A host starts returning 429/5xx (or its robots.txt suddenly disallows you).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The crawler applies host-level backoff (raises next_fetch_at, slows that politeness bucket) and re-reads/respects the cached robots policy; it does not keep hammering.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-host backoff + retry budget with exponential delay; robots cache with TTL; give up after N failed attempts and lower priority.

failureDrills.scenario: A crawler trap (infinite calendar / faceted links) floods the frontier.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Depth and per-host URL caps plus duplicate-cluster detection stop the explosion; the trap's near-identical pages are deduped by content hash.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-host URL budget, max crawl depth, content-hash near-dup clustering, and trap heuristics on URL patterns.

flows.name: Crawl loop with seen/dedup check
flows.note: The crawler pulls a polite URL from the frontier, skips it if already seen, otherwise fetches, stores, and enqueues newly discovered links.

steps.title: 3. Build the Inverted Index
description: To answer 'which docs contain these words' fast, build an inverted index: for each term, a posting list of the documents (and positions) containing it — the opposite of scanning every document.

The indexing pipeline parses each stored doc, assigns a compact internal docID, tokenizes/normalizes terms (lowercase, stemming), and writes postings. Concretely: a term dictionary maps each term to its posting list; postings are delta-encoded and compressed (docIDs are sorted and gap-encoded) so a multi-PB corpus stays affordable; and per-block skip lists / block-max metadata let query execution skip whole chunks of a posting list, which is why a posting-list lookup is fast even for common terms. Indexing is a batch/stream pipeline that produces immutable index segments, periodically merged.
The same pass also extracts each page's outbound links to build the link graph, which feeds the query-independent page scores (PageRank) used later at ranking time.

view.caption: The indexing pipeline reads pages from the document store, writes postings into the inverted index shards, and updates the link graph.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why an inverted index rather than scanning documents for the query terms?

concepts.definition: term -> list of documents (with positions/frequencies) containing it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a corpus scan into a posting-list lookup and intersection.

whyNow: The inverted index is the core data structure of search; everything in serving operates on its posting lists.

recap.after: Term posting lists + a link graph.
recap.newRisk: The index is far too large for one machine — it must be sharded, and queries must hit all shards.

steps.title: 4. Shard the Index and Scatter-Gather Queries
description: The index spans petabytes, so partition it by document: each shard holds the full inverted index for its slice of the corpus. (Document partitioning beats term partitioning here — each shard answers independently and load is even.)

A query is scattered to all shards in parallel; each returns its top-k candidates for the query, and an aggregator merges them. Replicate each shard for QPS and availability — concretely ~10K primary shards (~10M docs each) x ~3 replicas. 'Scatter to all shards' means hitting one chosen replica per primary partition, not broadcasting to every replica.

The basic path is: parse/rewrite the query -> scatter to one replica per partition -> per-shard top-k retrieval -> merge -> re-rank -> hydrate snippets. That is the clean partitioning lesson. Making that scatter-gather survive 100K QPS — broker fan-out topology, deadlines, hedging, top-k limits, partial results, and overload behavior — is involved enough that it gets its own sub-step (4b).

view.caption: The searcher's query reaches the query service, which checks the result cache and otherwise scatters it across the inverted index shards.

options.name: Document partitioning (default)
options.description: The corpus is split by document across shards, each holding complete postings for its docs, so every query fans out to all shards and merges results.
pros: Each shard holds complete postings for its docs, so any shard answers any query independently.
Load and latency are balanced — every query fans out evenly to all shards.
Adding corpus capacity is just adding shards; multi-term queries need no cross-shard joins.
cons: Every query touches every shard, so total cost scales with shard count.
Tail latency is set by the slowest shard in the scatter.
A rare term still queries all shards even though few hold it.

view.caption: The query service scatters the query to every document-partitioned shard and gathers their matches, checking the result cache first.

options.name: Term partitioning
options.description: The index is split by term across shards, so each shard owns whole-term postings and a single-term query hits just the shard owning that term.
pros: Each shard owns whole-term postings, so a single-term query hits just one shard.
Less total work for queries whose terms live on few shards.
Postings for a term are colocated, simplifying that term's scoring.
cons: A multi-term query must intersect postings across shards, shipping large lists between nodes.
Hot/common terms ('the', a trending word) overload their owning shard — severe skew.
Harder to balance and to add capacity, since load follows term popularity, not doc count.

view.caption: The query service routes the query to the term-partitioned shards holding its terms and intersects their postings, checking the result cache first.

steps.decisionPrompt: Partition the index by document or by term? What does each do to a multi-term query?

concepts.definition: Each shard holds complete postings for a subset of documents.
concepts.whyItMatters: Every shard answers any query independently; load and latency are balanced.
concepts.example: 10K shards each index ~10M docs; a query fans out to all, top-k from each.
concepts.group: Shard the Index and Scatter-Gather Queries

concepts.definition: Fan a query to all shards, collect partial results, and merge.
concepts.whyItMatters: Parallelizes search across the whole corpus within the latency budget.
concepts.example: Aggregator merges 10K x top-100 into a global candidate set.
concepts.group: Shard the Index and Scatter-Gather Queries

whyNow: Sharded scatter-gather is how search scales to billions of docs while staying sub-second; the document-vs-term partitioning choice is the key senior decision.

recap.after: Document-sharded, replicated index served via basic scatter-gather.
recap.newRisk: Fanning out every cache-miss query to 10K shards is an enormous load — the serving path has to be hardened to survive 100K QPS (next sub-step).

flows.name: Scatter-gather query across shards
flows.note: The query service fans out to all document-partitioned shards in parallel and merges their per-shard top-k; a slow shard returns partial results rather than blowing the latency budget.

steps.title: 4a. Document vs Term Partitioning
description: How you shard the index is a fork with big consequences. Document partitioning gives each shard the full index for a slice of documents: every shard can answer any query independently, load is even, and a multi-term query just runs locally per shard — but every query fans out to all shards (scatter-gather).

Term partitioning splits by term (a shard owns certain terms' posting lists): a single-term query hits one shard, but a multi-term query must ship and intersect huge posting lists across shards, and popular terms create hotspots.

Web search uses document partitioning as the default — the scatter-gather cost is predictable and parallelizable, whereas term partitioning's cross-shard intersections and hotspots are worse at this scale. Real systems still add nuance on top: tiered indexes (a small hot tier served first), hybrid schemes, and special handling for very common terms — so 'document-partition everything' is the right starting point, not the whole story.

view.caption: The query service fans a query out to the inverted index shards to compare document- versus term-partitioned scatter-gather.

steps.decisionPrompt: Partition the index by document or by term? What does a 3-word query cost in each?

concepts.definition: Doc-partition = each shard has full postings for its docs (scatter-gather, even load); term-partition = shards own terms (single-term cheap, multi-term cross-shard expensive).
concepts.whyItMatters: It determines query fan-out, load balance, and hotspot behavior at web scale.
concepts.example: Document partitioning + scatter-gather is the dominant web-search retrieval pattern (e.g. Elasticsearch shards, large web indexes) — though real systems layer tiering and hot-term handling on top.

traps.trap: Choosing term partitioning because single-term queries look cheap.
traps.why: Real queries are multi-term; intersecting posting lists across shards is expensive and popular terms hotspot.

recap.after: Document partitioning chosen for even load and parallel scatter-gather.
recap.newRisk: Every query touches all shards — tail latency is the slowest shard (addressed with replicas/hedging).

steps.title: 4b. Make Scatter-Gather Survive 100K QPS
description: Document partitioning is settled; now make the fan-out economical. The headline number is brutal: ~30K cache-miss QPS x ~10K shards = ~300M shard-requests/s, and each cache-miss query gathers ~10MB of top-k across the fleet. A single QuerySvc box opening 10K connections per query cannot do this — the serving topology has to absorb it.

Broker topology. Many stateless query frontends terminate user requests; each hands a parsed query to a broker that fans out. At 10K shards you don't fan out flat from one process — use a two-level (or n-ary) fan-out tree of broker workers, so each broker talks to a few hundred shards and an intermediate tier merges sub-results. This bounds per-process connections and lets merge happen hierarchically (early pruning at each level) instead of one box sorting a 10M-candidate set.

Per-replica load. ~30K cache-miss QPS x 10K shards spread over 3 replicas/shard is ~10K shard-requests/s per index server — that sets the per-server CPU/IO budget. If a shard's replicas can't hold ~10K req/s of top-k retrieval, you add replicas (hot shards get more) or treat each 'shard' as a logical partition spread across several serving nodes. Network: ~10MB gathered per miss x 30K miss QPS is hundreds of GB/s of internal egress, which is why top-k is hierarchically merged and pruned, not shipped whole to one aggregator.

Top-k discipline. Default to a small per-shard top-k (~100). 10K shards x top-100 is ~1M candidates before merge; phase-2 re-ranks only the top ~1000. Larger top-k (up to ~1000/shard) is reserved for rare queries that need deep recall — it is a deliberate, not default, cost because it multiplies both merge work and network.

Deadlines, hedging, partial results. The broker propagates a deadline so shards self-abort; it hedges a duplicate request to a second replica when the first is slow; and it drops shards that miss the deadline, returning partial results rather than blowing the sub-second budget.

Overload behavior. A 60-70% result-cache hit rate is what makes 100K QPS affordable, but it is not a complete overload story. Under a cache-miss burst the system also needs admission control / load shedding (shed or queue excess scatter-gathers), aggressive partial results (lower the deadline), hot-query precomputation and cache warming, and query classification (cheap path for common queries). A real 100K-QPS web search also tiers the index (a small hot tier served first) so most queries never touch the full fan-out.

view.caption: Stateless query frontends check the result cache; on a miss a broker fan-out tree scatters to one replica per partition with deadlines and hedging, and merges hierarchically.

steps.decisionPrompt: 300M shard-requests/s on cache misses — what broker topology, per-replica load, top-k, and overload controls keep that sub-second?

concepts.definition: A two-level (or n-ary) tree of broker workers that each fan out to a few hundred shards and merge sub-results hierarchically.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds per-process connections and merge cost so no single box opens 10K connections or sorts a 10M-candidate set.
concepts.example: Root broker -> ~32 intermediate brokers -> ~300 shards each; merge + prune at every level.

concepts.definition: Send a per-request deadline shards self-abort on, and duplicate slow shard requests to a second replica.
concepts.whyItMatters: Total latency is the slowest shard; deadlines + hedging bound the tail and yield partial results instead of timeouts.
concepts.example: Hedge after p95; drop shards past the deadline and serve partial results.

whyNow: Once the capacity model reveals 300M shard-requests/s, the design has to prove the serving fleet can absorb it — this is the senior/staff core of the case.

recap.after: A broker fan-out tree with per-replica budgets, top-k discipline, deadlines/hedging, partial results, and overload controls.
recap.newRisk: Merged candidates still need good ranking; and at this QPS even cheap fan-out is heavy, so caching is mandatory.

bottlenecks.issue: Tail latency: the slowest shard sets query latency across a 10K-way fan-out.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Replicas + hedged requests; per-shard deadline + cap on top-k work; skip slow shards with partial results; hierarchical merge so no single node sorts everything.

bottlenecks.issue: Fan-out amplification: cache-miss QPS x shard count is huge (~30K x 10K ~= 300M shard-requests/s, ~10MB gathered per miss).
bottlenecks.mitigation: High result-cache hit rate to cut miss QPS; broker fan-out tree to bound connections; small default per-shard top-k with early pruning; index tiering and admission control / load shedding under bursts.

bottlenecks.issue: Per-replica saturation: ~10K shard-requests/s per index server at 3x replication.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Add replicas to hot shards, or spread a logical shard over multiple serving nodes; balanced document partitioning so per-replica load is even.

failureDrills.scenario: One replica goes slow (GC pause, hot shard) and threatens the deadline.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The broker's hedge request to a second replica returns first; the slow shard's late reply is ignored. If no replica answers in time, that shard is dropped and the query returns partial results (partialResults=true, timedOutShards>0).
failureDrills.mitigation: Request hedging + deadline propagation + partial-result merge; per-shard top-k cap to bound shard work.

failureDrills.scenario: Result-cache node fails and a burst of popular queries all miss at once (cache stampede).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: A single-flight / request-coalescing guard lets one query do the scatter-gather while duplicates wait for its result; admission control sheds excess miss traffic; serving degrades in load, not correctness.
failureDrills.mitigation: Single-flight per query key, cache warming for top queries, TTL jitter so entries don't expire in lockstep, and load shedding on the cache-miss path.

failureDrills.scenario: A cache-miss burst pushes scatter QPS past fleet capacity.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Admission control sheds or queues excess scatter-gathers and the broker tightens the deadline (more partial results) so latency for admitted queries stays bounded, rather than every query degrading together.
failureDrills.mitigation: Load shedding / admission control, tighter deadlines under pressure, hot-query precomputation, index tiering so most queries hit the hot tier, and per-query-class budgets.

flows.name: Hierarchical broker fan-out under deadline
flows.note: A query frontend checks the result cache; on a miss a broker tree fans out to one replica per partition with a deadline, hedges slow replicas, and merges hierarchically, dropping shards that miss the deadline.

messages.label: fan-out tree: scatter w/ deadline (one replica/partition)
hedge slow replica; drop shards past deadline

steps.title: 5. Rank Results
description: Relevance is more than 'contains the words'. Use a two-phase approach: each shard cheaply retrieves and scores candidates (e.g. TF-IDF/BM25 text relevance), then the aggregator re-ranks the merged candidate set with a richer model.

Blend signals: text relevance, query-independent page score (link-graph authority like PageRank), freshness, and a spam/quality score. The expensive re-rank runs only on the small merged candidate set, not the whole corpus.

Defending quality is not optional for web search: a per-doc spam/quality score (computed offline from link patterns, content heuristics, and known-bad signals, stored on the document and looked up at re-rank time) demotes link farms and low-quality pages, and suppressed/takedown docs are filtered out entirely. Freshness scoring biases recent content up for time-sensitive queries.
Query-independent scores (page authority and spam/quality) are precomputed offline from the link graph and content so they're a cheap lookup at query time.

Out of scope here (worth naming): ads, personalized ranking, semantic/vector retrieval, query understanding (synonyms/intent), and safe-search filtering depth — this case focuses on the core text + authority + quality ranking.

view.caption: The query service retrieves candidates from the index shards and sends them to the ranking service, which scores them using page authority from the link graph.

options.name: Two-phase: cheap recall + learned re-rank (default)
options.description: Phase-1 BM25 narrows matches to a small candidate set per shard, then a learned model re-ranks the merged top candidates using text, authority, freshness, and spam/quality signals.
pros: Phase-1 BM25 cheaply narrows 100K matches to a small candidate set per shard.
The expensive learned model runs only on the merged top candidates, bounding latency.
Blends text relevance, precomputed page authority, freshness, and a spam/quality score for strong, abuse-resistant ordering.
cons: A good document can be cut in phase 1 before the re-ranker ever sees it (recall ceiling).
Two scoring stages and a learned model add pipeline and serving complexity.
The re-rank model needs training data and ongoing maintenance.

view.caption: The query service pulls cheap candidates from the index shards, then the ranking service re-ranks them with a learned model using link-graph scores.

options.name: Single-pass full scoring of all matches
options.description: One scoring stage runs the full ranking model over every matching document, with no cheap recall pass to narrow the set first.
pros: No recall ceiling — every matching document is scored by the full model.
Simpler pipeline: one scoring stage, no candidate hand-off.
Ranking quality is uniform across the whole match set.
cons: Scoring all 100K matches with a rich model per query is far too expensive at 100K QPS.
Blows the sub-second latency budget the scatter-gather is built around.
Wastes work — only the top handful of results are ever shown.

view.caption: The query service hands every matching document from the index shards to the ranking service to score in a single pass.

options.name: Static link-authority ordering (PageRank only)
options.description: Results are ordered by a query-independent page-authority score precomputed offline from the link graph and looked up at query time.
pros: Query-independent page score is precomputed offline — a cheap lookup at query time.
Very fast: no per-query relevance model to run.
Link authority gives a baseline quality signal — genuinely well-linked pages rank above obscure ones.
cons: Ignores query-specific text relevance, so results match authority more than intent.
Same authoritative pages float up regardless of the actual query terms.
Vulnerable to link manipulation: link farms and SEO abuse game the graph, which is exactly why explicit spam/quality signals are needed on top of PageRank.
Authority scores lag the live graph until the next recompute.

view.caption: The query service retrieves matches from the index shards and orders them purely by precomputed authority from the link graph.

steps.decisionPrompt: You have 100K matching docs. How do you rank them well without scoring all 100K expensively?

concepts.definition: Cheap per-shard candidate scoring, then expensive re-rank of the merged top candidates.
concepts.whyItMatters: Spends ranking cost only where it matters, keeping latency bounded.
concepts.example: BM25 to get top-1000, then a learned model to order the final 10.

concepts.definition: A precomputed per-doc authority score (e.g. PageRank) independent of the query.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adds a strong relevance signal as a cheap lookup.
concepts.example: Blend BM25 with PageRank at re-rank time.

whyNow: Ranking is what makes results useful; two-phase ranking is the standard way to combine quality and latency.

recap.after: Two-phase ranked results blending text + authority.
recap.newRisk: At 100K QPS even cheap scatter-gather is heavy — need caching and freshness handling.

flows.name: Two-phase ranking
flows.note: Cheap per-shard candidate scoring, then an expensive re-rank of only the merged candidate set, blending text relevance with precomputed page authority.

steps.title: 6. Caching for QPS and Latency
description: Query popularity is heavily skewed: a small set of queries recurs constantly. Cache full results for popular queries so repeats skip scatter-gather entirely. Assume a ~60-70% hit rate on the result cache — that is what turns ~100K QPS into only ~30-40K QPS actually scattering to shards, which is what makes the fleet size affordable.
Cache hot document metadata/snippets so result hydration doesn't refetch per query. Both caches cut both latency and load.

Bound cache staleness with TTLs (short for fresh/news-like queries, longer for stable ones) so fresh content still surfaces; invalidate or expire result-cache entries when a new index segment publishes documents matching those terms. Protect against stampede: when a hot entry expires, a single-flight guard lets one request recompute while duplicates wait, and TTL jitter avoids synchronized expiry. Warm the cache for the top queries after a big crawl/segment publish.

Operate the caches by metric: alert on result-cache and doc-cache hit rate (a sudden drop signals a stampede or eviction storm), single-flight wait/coalesce rate, stale-hit rate, and invalidation lag after a segment publish. A hit-rate collapse is the early warning that the scatter-gather fleet is about to be overloaded.

view.caption: The searcher's query hits the query service, which serves whole results from the result cache and hydrates snippets from the doc cache, falling back to the index shards.

steps.decisionPrompt: What fraction of queries are repeats, and how does that change your capacity plan?
whyNow: Caching is how the QPS target is actually met affordably; recognizing query skew is the practical insight.

concepts.definition: Caching the full ranked result for a (popular) query string.
concepts.whyItMatters: Repeated queries skip the entire scatter-gather + rank path.
concepts.example: Top trending queries served from cache with a short TTL.

recap.after: Popular queries and hot docs served from cache.
recap.newRisk: Stale results vs freshness; the index must keep updating as the crawl finds new content.

steps.title: 7. Freshness and Continuous Indexing
description: The crawl never stops, so the index must update continuously. Indexing produces immutable segments that are added and periodically merged; serving reads the current segment set, so new content appears without rebuilding the whole index.

Changes and deletes need real semantics, not just appends. Because segments are immutable, an updated page is written as a new version in a new segment and the old version is marked in a tombstone list so serving skips it; deletions, redirects, and takedowns/spam suppression are the same tombstone mechanism. The serving visibility contract is generation-based: an indexer builds a segment, validates it, then flips a publish marker that bumps the generation; readers atomically refresh to the latest published generation, so a query sees a consistent segment set. A bad segment is handled by not publishing it (or rolling the generation back), and background merge compacts small segments and drops tombstoned docs.

Prioritize freshness in the crawl frontier (re-crawl fast-changing or important pages sooner, driven by observed change frequency) and propagate new segments to shards quickly so breaking content is searchable within a bounded time. Tie result-cache invalidation to segment publishes that touch matching terms/docs, not a vague 'material' rule.
Recompute query-independent scores (link graph / PageRank) periodically as the graph evolves; serve with the latest available scores.

Operate the pipeline by metric: alert on segment build lag, validation failure rate, the live publish generation per shard, merge backlog, tombstone ratio, and rollback count. A stuck publish generation or a growing merge backlog means freshness is silently degrading even while serving looks healthy.

view.caption: The crawler and frontier keep discovering pages into the document store while the indexing pipeline continuously writes postings to the index shards and updates the link graph.

options.name: Segment-based incremental indexing (default)
options.description: New documents are written as small immutable segments served immediately, with background merging keeping segment count bounded without blocking serving.
pros: New docs become a small immutable segment served immediately — no full rebuild.
A breaking news article is searchable within minutes of crawl.
Background merging keeps the segment count bounded without blocking serving.
cons: Many small segments slow queries until merged (read amplification).
Segment merging consumes background CPU/IO that competes with serving.
Per-doc authority scores still lag until the link graph is recomputed.

view.caption: The crawler-frontier loop feeds the indexer, which adds new immutable segments to the index shards and updates the link graph without a full rebuild.

options.name: Periodic full index rebuild
options.description: The entire index is rebuilt from scratch on a schedule and swapped in atomically, with authority scores recomputed together each build.
pros: Each build produces one clean, optimally-merged index — no small-segment overhead at query time.
Authority scores and the index are recomputed together, so they stay consistent.
Simple pipeline: build, swap, serve; no live-segment bookkeeping.
cons: New content isn't searchable until the next full build — hours, not minutes.
Rebuilding a petabyte-scale index is hugely expensive to run repeatedly.
Fails the 'searchable minutes after publication' freshness goal.

view.caption: The indexing pipeline periodically reprocesses the whole document store to rebuild the inverted index shards from scratch.

steps.decisionPrompt: How does a brand-new news article become searchable minutes after publication?

concepts.definition: Build small immutable index segments, add them live, merge in the background.
concepts.whyItMatters: New content is searchable without rebuilding the full index.
concepts.example: Fresh docs -> new segment -> shard serves it immediately; merged later.

whyNow: Closing on freshness shows search is a living pipeline, not a one-time build — tying crawl priority and incremental indexing to the freshness requirement.

recap.newRisk: Segment merge cost and score-recompute lag — bounded by background merging and incremental updates.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: A replica serves it; the shard can be rebuilt from the document store / segments.
failureDrills.mitigation: Shard replication + rebuildable index from durable docs.

failureDrills.scenario: A bad segment is built (corrupt postings / quality regression) during a high-news crawl.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Validation fails the segment so the publish marker never advances to it, or the generation is rolled back; readers keep serving the last good generation. Stale result-cache entries are flushed on the eventual good publish.
failureDrills.mitigation: Build -> validate -> atomic publish-marker flip with rollback; generation-based reader refresh; cache invalidation tied to publishes.

failureDrills.scenario: A new ranker model or quality-score recompute regresses result quality after rollout.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The ranker version is a tracked control-plane value; quality metrics (spam demotion rate, click/feedback indicators if in scope) flag the regression and the serving fleet is rolled back to the previous model version, decoupled from segment generation so ranking can revert without re-publishing the index.
failureDrills.mitigation: Versioned ranker models with canary/shadow evaluation before full rollout, quality-metric alerts, and one-flip rollback to the last good model version; flush/repopulate result cache on the revert.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Web Search Engine
finalDesign.description: A crawler fleet pulls from a politeness-aware URL frontier (canonicalization, robots cache, host backoff, recrawl by change frequency), dedups by URL/content, and stores raw pages. An indexing pipeline parses docs into a document-partitioned inverted index (~10K shards x ~3 replicas) of immutable, generation-versioned segments, and builds a link graph for query-independent page and spam/quality scores. The query service parses/rewrites the query, checks a result cache (~60-70% hit), otherwise a broker scatters to one replica per partition with deadline propagation and hedging, gathers per-shard top-k, and a ranker re-ranks the merged set blending text relevance, page authority, freshness, and quality (suppressing spam/takedown docs); snippets hydrate from a doc cache, with partial results when a shard misses its deadline. Popular queries and hot docs are cached (single-flight + TTL jitter against stampede); the crawl + segment-based indexing run continuously, publishing new generations atomically so new content is searchable within a bounded time and bad segments can be rolled back. Explicitly out of scope: ads, personalized ranking, semantic/vector retrieval, query suggestions, image/video search, and legal takedown workflow.

view.caption: The frontier-driven crawler fills the document store and seen-URL store; the indexer builds the inverted index shards and link graph; and the searcher's query flows through the query service, result and doc caches, index shards, and ranking service.

functional.how: Query parse/rewrite turns phrases and +/-/OR operators into posting-list intersections/unions; positions in postings support phrase ("exact") matching.

nonFunctional.how: Parallel scatter-gather + broker deadlines/hedging/partial results + caching + bounded per-shard work.
Shard replication + broker fan-out tree + per-replica budgets + overload controls + result/doc caches.
Per-host rate limits + robots in the frontier.

say: Split the problem into crawl, index, and serve.
Pin sub-second latency over billions of docs and high QPS.
Note politeness and freshness as real constraints.
Crawler + URL frontier feeding a document store.
Inverted index built by an indexing pipeline.
Document vs term partitioning and scatter-gather tail latency.
Two-phase ranking blending text relevance and page authority.
Caching for QPS; continuous segment-based indexing for freshness.
Tradeoffs: partitioning scheme, ranking cost vs latency, freshness vs cache staleness.
Mention spam/quality and personalization as extensions.

expectations: Crawls and builds an inverted index.
May miss sharding, scatter-gather, and ranking depth.
Reasons about partitioning tradeoffs and tail latency (hedging).
Designs incremental segment indexing for freshness.
Discusses ranking signal blending and caching under query skew precisely.

followUps: Document partitioning vs term partitioning — when would term partitioning win?
How do you control scatter-gather tail latency across thousands of shards?
How would you detect and demote web spam / low-quality pages?
How do you incorporate personalization or query understanding (synonyms, intent)?
How would you make a brand-new page searchable within seconds, not minutes?
